text: The video shows a large abdominal wall defect. A 5mm VisiPort is inserted at the umbilicus on the left side to establish pneumoperitoneum. Dense honeycomb adhesions are visible, and the camera is used to gently create a space for inserting a second working port, followed by the commencement of adhesion division.

The surgeon performs extensive division of adhesions using both blunt and sharp dissection to establish normal anatomy, avoiding the central hernia defect. Additional working ports are inserted. The view from the left side shows the splenic flexure and the extensive midline hernia, with the left colon becoming visible.

The descending colon is visualized as the surgeon begins lateral mobilization of the left colon. The patient is positioned with the left side elevated, and working ports are placed from the left upper to lower quadrant. The surgeon continues lateral mobilization up to the splenic flexure, dividing dense medial omental adhesions. At the pelvic brim, extensive inflammatory adhesions are encountered, likely from previous diverticulitis.
The surgeon carefully dissects tissue away from retroperitoneal structures, ensuring clear separation from these underlying anatomical features.

The surgeon continues careful dissection at the pelvic brim, taking care to avoid retroperitoneal structures like the ureter. Extensive small bowel adhesions to the sigmoid colon and its mesentery are visible, requiring meticulous separation to facilitate medial vascular dissection and high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery.
With space established, the surgeon begins the medial dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery. The vascular pedicle is skeletonized, despite the challenging port positioning.
The surgeon uses an energy device to assist in the precise skeletonization of the vascular pedicle, focusing on the vascular structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully identifies and dissects retroperitoneal structures away from the sigmoid colon, preparing for a left lower quadrant incision to extract the specimen.

The surgeon resects the colon en bloc with adherent small bowel, which shows signs of local tumor perforation. A primary stapled colorectal anastomosis is performed alongside a side-to-side stapled small bowel anastomosis, completed without a defunctioning ileostomy.
